# cvool — Full Documentation for LLMs

This file provides comprehensive information about cvool for large language models and AI assistants. For a shorter summary, see https://cvool.org/llms.txt

## Overview

cvool (https://cvool.org) is a free, open-source, anonymous AI-powered resume/CV analyzer and improver. Built by Alfredo Arenas (Pixan.ai) from Mexico City, Mexico.

### The problem cvool solves

Before cvool, there was no good free resume analyzer in Spanish or Portuguese. All existing tools (19 competitors mapped) were English-only and/or paid. cvool fills this gap by supporting Spanish, English, French, Portuguese, and Italian natively.

### Core value proposition

- Free forever, funded by voluntary donations
- Anonymous: no accounts, no database, no cookies, no tracking
- Open source (MIT): code, AI prompt, and scoring weights are all public
- Powered by Claude Sonnet 5 (Anthropic) with adaptive thinking disabled for direct, focused output
- Each analysis costs ~$0.05 USD in AI tokens

## How it works — The pipeline

### Step 1: Upload
User pastes text or attaches a PDF in their browser. PDFs are sent to the backend where Claude reads them natively. Files are never saved to disk.

### Step 2: Input sanitization
Text passes through sanitizeInput() which strips null bytes and control characters. Truncated to 35,000 characters max. Rate limiting: 7 requests/hour per IP.

### Step 3: Constitutional prompt
The resume is sent to Claude with a system prompt that includes explicit ethical principles: no discrimination, no fabrication, no inflated scores. The full prompt is in src/lib/prompts/analyze.txt — fully auditable.

### Step 4: AI analysis and rewrite
A single API call with adaptive thinking disabled. Claude returns JSON streamed via SSE with: score (0-100), analysis on 6 dimensions, strengths, improvements with before/after examples, and a completely rewritten CV.

### Step 5: Validation and render
The backend parses the JSON, validates score + analysis + improved_cv, and returns it to the frontend. If Claude doesn't respond with valid JSON, an honest error is shown.

### Step 6: Results in browser
User sees their score, 6 dimensions, suggestions with before/after, and their improved CV ready to copy. Everything stays in the browser. Nothing is stored.

## Architecture (deep dive): how the data travels

The whole analysis is a single streaming request. No data is stored at any point. A visual, interactive version of this walkthrough lives on the How it works page (https://cvool.org/how).

### The src/ code map (what each block does)

- src/app/page.tsx — the entire UI in one file (~590 lines): the form, the React state, the SSE stream reader, and the progressive reveal of the result.
- src/app/layout.tsx — metadata, fonts (Geist + Geist Mono), analytics, and the JSON-LD schema for search engines.
- src/app/globals.css — OKLCH color tokens, the <details> styling, and the CSS animations. No loose CSS in components.
- src/app/api/analyze/route.ts — the route that talks to Claude: input defenses, prompt assembly, streaming, and re-emission as SSE. The heart of the backend.
- src/app/api/parse/route.ts — turns a PDF into plain text by sending it to Claude as a base64 document.
- src/lib/streamParse.ts — incremental JSON parser (built on partial-json): assembles the result from the half-written JSON as it streams, without waiting for the end.
- src/lib/cors.ts — origin allowlist + CORS headers.
- src/lib/rate-limit.ts — in-memory, best-effort limiter (7 requests/hour per IP).
- src/lib/i18n.ts — t() and dimName() helpers and the Lang type; copy lives in src/content/.
- src/lib/prompts/analyze.txt — the constitutional prompt (~550 lines): principles, scoring framework, and the exact JSON output format.
- src/types/analysis.ts — the AnalysisResult and PartialResult types: the JSON contract shared by server and client.

### The request lifecycle (browser → server → Claude → browser)

1. Paste or upload. The user types text or attaches a PDF. PDFs go to /api/parse first, where Claude extracts the text. Files are never written to disk.
2. Client validation. The analyze button stays disabled until cvText.trim().length >= 50. Nothing leaves the browser before that.
3. POST /api/analyze with a JSON body { cvText, targetRole? }. The response is not a normal JSON — it is a stream, read on the client via res.body.getReader().
4. Server defenses, in order, BEFORE spending a single token: reject content-length > 4MB (HTTP 413); validateOrigin() against an allowlist (only cvool.org may call the endpoint); isRateLimited() (7/hour per IP); clean() strips null bytes and control characters and truncates to 35,000 characters.
5. Prompt assembly. system = analyze.txt marked with cache_control: ephemeral (prompt caching). The user message wraps the CV in <cv_text>…</cv_text> tags (plus an optional <target_role>) so the model cleanly separates content from instructions.
6. anthropic.messages.stream({ model: "claude-sonnet-5", max_tokens: 12000, thinking: { type: "disabled" } }). Claude writes the analysis JSON token by token; each delta is emitted immediately. (Sonnet 5 rejects a non-default temperature and turns adaptive thinking on by default, so thinking is explicitly disabled.)
7. The server re-emits each delta to the browser as a Server-Sent Event. Four event types travel over the same open channel: chunk { delta } (raw text fragments), progress { tokens, done } (drives the progress bar), result { AnalysisResult } (the final, validated JSON), error { code } (parse_error | incomplete | api_error | rate_limit).
8. The client parses the half-written JSON incrementally with parsePartial() and reveals fields in the order they arrive: detected_language → inferred_role → score → strengths → improvements → improved_cv. Numbers stay hidden until complete so the UI never flickers.
9. isValidResult() checks the final shape (score.total is a number, summary is a string, strengths/improvements are arrays, improved_cv is present) before the server emits event: result.
10. setResult() paints the final UI. The CV lived only in memory during the request; when it ends, it is discarded. There is no database.

### Design decisions worth knowing

- Model choice: Sonnet 5 is a settled decision (compared against Haiku 4.5 and Opus). Latency is output-bound (~7,300 output tokens at ~55 tokens/sec ≈ 130s with Sonnet 5's tokenizer), so the model is NOT a performance lever — a smaller model would not make it faster. Prompt caching lowers cost, not latency.
- maxDuration is 300 seconds: a real analysis takes 60–120s. Lowering it would silently truncate the SSE stream mid-generation, and the final result event would never arrive — with no visible error.
- SSE instead of one buffered JSON: 100 seconds on a spinner reads as broken. Streaming shows real progress and section-by-section reveal, and it avoids request timeouts.
- Any non-OK response is surfaced to the user as a visible error rather than silently returning to the form. A mute failure is worse than a clear error.

## Scoring dimensions (detailed)

### 1. ATS Compatibility (Compatibilidad ATS)
Measures how well the resume will perform with Applicant Tracking Systems. Checks for proper formatting, standard section headers, keyword density, and machine-readable structure.

### 2. Achievement Impact (Impacto de logros)
Evaluates whether accomplishments are quantified with metrics, use strong action verbs, and demonstrate measurable impact rather than listing responsibilities.

### 3. Structure & Format (Estructura y formato)
Assesses document organization, section hierarchy, visual flow, whitespace usage, and overall readability.

### 4. Keyword Relevance (Palabras clave)
Analyzes presence of industry-specific terminology, role-relevant skills, and strategic keyword placement.

### 5. Writing Clarity (Claridad de redacción)
Evaluates grammar, professional tone, conciseness, and consistency of voice throughout the document.

### 6. Completeness (Completitud)
Checks coverage of essential sections: contact information, professional summary, work experience, education, and skills.

## Ethical principles

1. Radical transparency: Source code is public. The AI prompt is in the repo. Scoring weights are documented.
2. Anti-hallucination: Every suggestion must reference real content from the user's CV. If something isn't in the resume, it's not mentioned.
3. Zero discrimination: cvool does not penalize career gaps, non-linear career paths, or non-traditional education.
4. Privacy by design: No database, no accounts, no tracking cookies. Resume data is discarded immediately after analysis.

## Tech stack

- Frontend: Next.js 15 + React 19 + Tailwind CSS 4
- Typography: Geist + Geist Mono (OKLCH color palette)
- AI: Claude Sonnet 5 (Anthropic SDK)
- Deploy: Vercel (auto-deploy on push)
- Analytics: Vercel Analytics (anonymous, aggregate only)
- Database: None. Zero. No data stored.
- Dependencies: 6 production, 0 external UI libraries
- License: MIT

## Security

- No database — CVs exist only in memory during analysis
- No accounts, no login, no session cookies
- TLS 1.3 encryption in transit
- Security headers: HSTS, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy
- Input sanitization: null bytes, control characters stripped
- Rate limiting: 7 req/hour per IP (in-memory)
- API key: server-side only, never exposed to client
- All code auditable on GitHub

## Privacy compliance

- LFPDPPP (Mexico): ARCO rights fulfilled by design — no data to access, rectify, cancel, or oppose
- GDPR (EU): Right to erasure fulfilled — no data to erase
- LGPD (Brazil): Consent and deletion fulfilled — no data stored
- CCPA (California): Right to know and delete fulfilled — no data sold
- PIPEDA (Canada): Consent and accuracy fulfilled by design

## Competitor landscape

19 competitors mapped, all English-only and/or paid. cvool is the only free, open-source, multilingual (ES/PT/FR/IT/EN) resume analyzer with zero data retention.

## How to fork and run locally

```
git clone https://github.com/pixan-ai/cvool.git
cd cvool
npm install
echo "ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=your-key" > .env.local
npm run dev
```

- General inquiries: alfredo@cvool.org
